aiTitle: Resume halted issue on re-run with preserved worktree

content.text:
{"id":"ABA-299","title":"Resume a halted issue on re-run (reuse the preserved worktree)","description":"## What

On re-run after a halt, drain-cycle will **reuse the preserved worktree** instead of failing on the existing branch, and spawn a **fresh** `claude` session whose prompt says "prior work is committed on this branch — review it and continue from where it left off." A fresh session means a **clean context, so the per-issue token budget resets** (the whole reason the operator raised the cap). On Done, the existing teardown removes the worktree as normal.

**Scope.** The change is deliberately narrow: automatic only for the clean post-halt state (registered worktree + branch); bounded by a resume-attempt cap; preserves a dirty tree as-is (never resets/cleans); not in-process auto-retry. The manual escape hatch (delete worktree + branch by hand) remains the way to force a fresh start.

## Why

### The mechanics today

`drain-cycle` spawns one `claude -p` worker per Linear issue in an isolated git worktree at `<target-repo>/.worktrees/<id>` on a branch named `<id>`. When a worker is SIGKILLed mid-issue (the dominant case: a per-issue **token-cap breach**), the orchestrator reverts the issue's Linear state to its pre-run value (e.g. `Todo`, so it is re-picked next invocation) but **leaves the worktree and branch in place** for inspection (`docs/design-decisions.md` §3).

### The gap

On re-run, the issue is re-picked, but `worktree.add` runs `git worktree add -b <id> …`, which fails because that branch already exists. Today this surfaces as a clean `Halt: … — setup failed: … a branch named '<id>' already exists` (the generic pre-spawn handler already turns the `RuntimeError` into spec-shaped UX — so the "detect cleanly" goal of the superseded <issue id="3c8d126f-d01d-4a8e-a7ac-faa69dc6027b">ABA-231</issue> is effectively met). But the run cannot proceed: the operator must manually delete the worktree/branch, **discarding any committed work the killed session produced**.

### Motivating case

A token-breached run on <issue id="f8927151-bb4d-4af7-9410-071b39c8577c">ABA-276</issue> had already committed 2 commits / 142 lines to the `ABA-276` branch before being killed. That work was the durable record of progress — and the only way forward was to throw it away and re-drain from scratch (re-spending \~8M tokens).

### The design decision this reverses

`docs/design-decisions.md` §8 currently pins: *"Retry. A halted issue is not retried automatically. The operator inspects the worktree and decides — fix, redo manually, or descope."* This work **partially reverses** that and so must add a new **§14** that supersedes the §8 "Retry" bullet (per the "memory lives in artefacts / ADRs are superseded, not silently ignored" rule). (§13 is already taken by "Opt-in OpenTelemetry tracing to Honeycomb", which landed after this issue was shaped — the next free section number is §14; the agent should confirm the highest section at implementation time.) The reversal is narrow and must be documented as such:

* Resume reuses a **preserved, committed** work-in-progress on a **manual re-run** (or scripted re-run). It is **not** in-process auto-retry, and it is **bounded** by a resume-attempt cap (below).
* §3 (worktree preservation on halt) and §10 (`link_project_config`) are **relied upon**, not changed.
* The `docs/tasks/rerun-after-halt-detect-cleanly.md` framing ("no auto-cleanup, operator owns retry; detect-and-halt only") is superseded by this work; note it in §14. (The corresponding Linear issue, <issue id="3c8d126f-d01d-4a8e-a7ac-faa69dc6027b">ABA-231</issue>, is already Canceled/archived — this issue replaces its stance.)

### Why not `claude --resume`

A SIGKILLed worker has `session_id: null` in the run log (it dies before the terminal `result` event), so `--resume` isn't even available. And `--resume` would reload the exact context that blew the token budget, re-breaching almost immediately. The committed branch — not the session — is the correct resume point.

### Assumptions (the core bet)

* **A1.** The dominant halt is a token-cap breach, and a killed worker has usually committed incremental work to the `<id>` branch (true in the <issue id="f8927151-bb4d-4af7-9410-071b39c8577c">ABA-276</issue> case: 2 commits). If a worker dies before committing anything, resume re-runs from an empty branch — no worse than today, and bounded by the cap (A3).
* **A2.** Only the clean post-halt state (registered worktree + branch) is worth auto-handling. Operator-induced partial states (branch without worktree, orphan dir) are rare and acceptably handled by the **existing** clean halt — no new code. This is the main simplification; if partial-state auto-recovery turns out to be wanted, it's an additive follow-up.
* **A3.** A fresh session, pointed at committed work and told to continue, will build on it rather than restart. This is the core bet; the resume prompt directive (`git log`/`git status` first) is the cheap insurance, and the resume-attempt cap is the backstop if the bet fails for a given issue.

## How

### Resolved design choices

* **Resume is automatic on re-run** (the operator's explicit intent). Forcing a fresh start is the manual escape hatch: delete the worktree + branch (`git worktree remove … && git branch -D <id>`), exactly as we just did for <issue id="f8927151-bb4d-4af7-9410-071b39c8577c">ABA-276</issue>. No new flag needed.
* **Resume-attempt cap = 3** (configurable). After 3 non-Done attempts of an issue within the cycle, drain-cycle halts **before spawning** (zero token burn) and leaves the issue for manual intervention. This is the only backstop: the cycle-wide token cap does **not** fire on repeated halts — the breach path `return 1`s before `check_cycle`, and each re-run is a new process with a fresh `RunLog`, so cumulative cycle tokens reset every invocation.
* **Dirty preserved tree: preserve as-is.** The fresh session reconciles uncommitted edits via `git status`/`git log`. Never `reset --hard`/`clean` (would destroy the saved work and the gitignored config symlinks). Never switch teardown to `--force`.

### Worktree reuse logic (two branches, not five)

For picked issue `<id>` in `target_repo`, with `path = target_repo/.worktrees/<id>`:

1. **A worktree is already registered at** `path` → reuse it. Run **no git command** — just return `(path, resumed=True)`. This is the clean post-halt state (the <issue id="f8927151-bb4d-4af7-9410-071b39c8577c">ABA-276</issue> case) and the only state worth auto-handling.
2. **Otherwise** → call today's `add` (`git worktree add -b <id> <path> main`), returning `(path, resumed=False)`.

That's it. The rare leftover states — branch exists but no worktree, or an orphan directory at `path` — make `add` raise `RuntimeError` (with git's stderr), which the orchestrator's **existing** pre-spawn handler already turns into the clean `Halt: … — setup failed: …` line. We do **not** add bespoke detection, `prune`, branch re-checkout, or a new exception type for those: they're operator-induced, rare, and the operator clears the leftover by hand (`git worktree remove … && git branch -D <id>`). **The detection is one git call** (`git worktree list --porcelain`, parsed for a record at `path.resolve()`) plus the existing `add`.

Verified against real git: any `git worktree add` onto an already-registered path fails, so the reuse case must **not** call `add` — hence the registration check first. Reuse is a pure no-op on git state, so a dirty index / staged / untracked files in the preserved worktree survive untouched (this is the "preserve as-is" choice, for free).

### Component changes

#### 1\. `drain_cycle/worktree.py` — new `ensure` (keep `add` as-is)

```python
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class WorktreeHandle:
    path: Path
    resumed: bool

def ensure(repo: Path, identifier: str) -> WorktreeHandle:
    """Reuse a preserved worktree if one is registered at the expected
    path, else create a fresh one via add(). Reuse runs no git command."""
    path = repo / WORKTREE_DIR / identifier
    if _is_registered_worktree(repo, path):
        return WorktreeHandle(path, resumed=True)
    return WorktreeHandle(add(repo, identifier), resumed=False)
```

* One private helper: `_is_registered_worktree(repo, path) -> bool` — runs `git worktree list --porcelain` (via the existing `_run_git`-style call) and checks for a `worktree` record equal to `path.resolve()`. No `WorktreeStateError`, no `prune`, no branch re-checkout, no `_branch_exists`.
* **Keep** `add(repo, identifier) -> Path` **exactly as-is** — `tests/test_worktree.py::test_add_failure_raises_with_git_stderr_in_message` pins it as the fail-on-duplicate primitive that `ensure` reuses for the fresh/leftover path, and its `RuntimeError` is what the orchestrator's existing handler turns into the clean halt.
* `link_project_config` is already idempotent (`os.path.lexists` skip); call it unconditionally after `ensure` for both fresh and resumed (confirmed safe — returns `[]` on reuse).

#### 2\. `drain_cycle/prompt.py` — `resumed` kwarg

```python
def build(issue: dict[str, Any], worktree: Path, *, resumed: bool = False) -> str: ...
```

* When `resumed=True`, prepend a resume segment as the **first line of the preamble** (after the `---` separator, before "Execution instructions:") so it leads the instructions but **does not displace** `_TAIL` **from last position** (the four-segment ordering is load-bearing). Text directs the agent to run `git log --oneline main..HEAD` and `git status` first and continue rather than restart.
* `resumed=False` default keeps every current call site and all existing `tests/test_prompt.py` assertions unchanged.

#### 3\. `drain_cycle/limits.py` — `max_resume_attempts`

* Add `max_resume_attempts: int | None = 3` to the `Limits` dataclass. It is a **count**, not a resource ceiling, but lives here because `limits.yml` is the natural config home; `null` disables. The existing `_coerce`/`load` path validates it (positive number or null; rejects bool/non-positive) with no change.

#### 4\. `drain_cycle/orchestrator.py` — swap `add`→`ensure`, thread flag, add cap guard

In the pre-spawn try-block (`orchestrator.py:169-205`), replace `worktree.add(...)` with `worktree.ensure(...)` and insert the cap check **before** `linear.set_state(..., "In Progress")` (no spawn ⇒ no Linear transition, mirroring the resolution-halt contract):

```python
handle = worktree.ensure(target_repo, identifier)   # RuntimeError on leftover/conflict → existing halt
worktree_path = handle.path
worktree.link_project_config(target_repo, worktree_path, repos.worktree_config_paths)
if handle.resumed and limits.max_resume_attempts is not None \
        and _resume_attempts(log.cycle_id, identifier) >= limits.max_resume_attempts:
    # clean halt BEFORE set_state: run-log entry (cached issue state), stderr Halt line, return 1.
    # No revert (state was never moved). Distinct reason names the cap.
    ...
linear.set_state(issue["id"], _IN_PROGRESS_STATE_NAME)
...
agent_prompt = prompt.build(issue, worktree_path, resumed=handle.resumed)
```

* New `_resume_attempts(cycle_id, identifier) -> int`: glob `runlog.runs_dir()/<cycle_id>-*.json` (reusing the per-cycle filename convention) and count entries with `issue_identifier == identifier` and `final_linear_state != "Done"` (prior non-Done attempts). The current run's just-created empty file contributes nothing.
* The `resumed` flag flows **only** to `prompt.build`, never to `worker.run_issue` — the worker always spawns a fresh session (`session_id` is null on kill; `--resume` is unavailable and undesirable).
* Cap-halt reuses the `_halt_message` helper with an appended reason, so the stderr line and the run-log `halt_reason` stay single-sourced.

#### 5\. Docs

* `docs/design-decisions.md`: add **§14** (resume on re-run) documenting the reversal of the §8 Retry bullet, the state matrix, the resume cap, the dirty-tree choice, and why `--resume` is not used. Annotate the §8 Retry bullet as superseded by §14. (Confirm the next free section number at implementation time — §13 is OpenTelemetry tracing.)
* `README.md`: the halt example currently says the worktree is "left in place"; add that a re-run now **resumes** it, and document `max_resume_attempts` alongside the other `limits.yml` keys.
* `docs/tasks/rerun-after-halt-detect-cleanly.md`: note it is superseded by this feature.

### Test plan

(Mirrors existing patterns: real git in tmp repos; monkeypatched Linear + fake-claude shell script; no conftest.) `pytest` **green is the per-PR-layer precondition** (see Delivery) — the unit and integration tests below are the layer-by-layer gate, distinct from the behavioural Acceptance criteria.

`tests/test_worktree.py` (real git, existing `_init_repo`):

* fresh → `WorktreeHandle(resumed=False)`, worktree+branch created.
* reuse registered worktree → call `ensure` once, commit + stage + leave untracked files, call `ensure` again → same path, `resumed=True`, and `git status --porcelain` / `git log` unchanged (pins the no-op-on-git guarantee).
* leftover branch without worktree (operator removed the worktree) → `ensure` falls through to `add`, which raises `RuntimeError` (confirms leftover states hit the existing halt path, not a crash).
* `link_project_config` idempotent on reuse → returns `[]`, symlink intact.

`tests/test_orchestrator_resume.py` (new; mirror `test_orchestrator_iteration.py`):

* **headline**: pre-seed worktree+branch+commit for the first issue, stub it `Todo`, fake-claude marks it Done → exit 0, **no worktree-add failure**, worktree removed on Done, entry `final_linear_state == "Done"`.
* resumed prompt mentions prior work: prompt-capturing fake-claude on a pre-seeded worktree → spawned prompt contains the resume directive and `<id>`.
* resume cap: pre-seed 3 prior non-Done entries for `<id>` in `runs/<cycle-id>-*.json`, `max_resume_attempts=3` → exit 1 with the cap halt reason, `set_state` not called, no spawn.

`tests/test_prompt.py`: `resumed=True` includes the resume directive + identifier and `_TAIL` is still the last non-empty line; `resumed=False` is byte-identical to today's prompt.

`tests/test_limits.py`: `max_resume_attempts` honours absent-key default (3), explicit `null` (off), positive override, and rejects bool/non-positive via `_coerce`.

### Task breakdown

1. `worktree.py`: `WorktreeHandle`, `_is_registered_worktree`, `ensure`. `add` untouched.
2. `tests/test_worktree.py`: reuse + leftover-falls-through tests — get git mechanics green first.
3. `prompt.py` + `tests/test_prompt.py`: resume variant.
4. `limits.py` + `tests/test_limits.py`: `max_resume_attempts`.
5. `orchestrator.py`: swap `add`→`ensure`, thread `resumed`, add `_resume_attempts` + cap guard.
6. `tests/test_orchestrator_resume.py`: integration tests.
7. `docs/design-decisions.md` §14, `README.md`, `rerun-after-halt-detect-cleanly.md` note.

## Delivery — author as a 4-PR Graphite stack

**This issue is not drained via drain-cycle and is not split into Linear sub-issues.** It is authored by **one supervised agent in a single session**, working on a branch off `main` in the repo root (not a `.worktrees/` sandbox), and delivered as a **Graphite (**`gt`**) PR stack** — one PR per layer, reviewed bottom→top. This is the owner-authorised exception to the AGENTS.md "push directly to main; PRs only when the owner asks" default. <issue id="21915a3a-c443-4c9e-a032-b0964f0ed845">ABA-299</issue> stays the single tracked issue.

**Precondition:** `gt` (1.8.x) authenticated against `origin` (`github.com/ababushkin/drain-cycle`); the session is supervised, so branch/PR creation runs with approvals. The repo has **no CI**, so each layer's gate is a local `pytest` run — not a remote check.

**The stack (maps the 7 tasks above onto 4 PRs):**

| PR | Layer | Tasks | Contents | Green on its own |
| -- | -- | -- | -- | -- |
| PR1 | worktree primitive | 1, 2 | `WorktreeHandle`, `_is_registered_worktree`, `ensure`; `test_worktree.py` reuse/leftover tests. `add` untouched. | yes (its unit tests) |
| PR2 | prompt variant | 3 | `prompt.build(resumed=)` + resume segment; `test_prompt.py`. | yes |
| PR3 | limits knob | 4 | `max_resume_attempts` on `Limits`; `test_limits.py`. | yes |
| PR4 | wiring + docs | 5, 6, 7 | orchestrator `add`→`ensure`, thread `resumed`, `_resume_attempts` + cap guard; `test_orchestrator_resume.py`; **and docs (§14, README, supersession note) in this same PR**. | yes (full suite) |

PR1–3 are independent leaves (different files), each green on its own even though no production caller uses them yet. PR4 is the only behaviour-changing layer and integrates the three below it. **Docs land in PR4** so the §8-reversal ADR ships in the same PR as the behaviour that reverses it (an architectural change updates its ADR in the same PR — don't trail the docs in a later PR).

**Authoring loop — one layer at a time, gated:**

1. `gt create` a branch for the layer (conventional-commit subject, e.g. `feat: worktree.ensure reuses a preserved worktree`).
2. Run `pytest` — **the layer's own tests must be green** before submitting (no CI; local pytest is the only gate).
3. `gt submit` — opens the real GitHub PR for that layer on `origin`.
4. **STOP and report the PR; wait for the operator's go-ahead.** Do **not** start the next layer until approved.
5. On approval, build the next layer **on top** with `gt create` (never branch from `main` for layers 2–4 — the stack is one lineage; `gt` sets the parent).
6. On review feedback for a lower layer: `gt down`, amend, `gt restack`, `gt submit` to update the stack. The same agent restacks the whole tower — it has full context, which is the reason one agent authors all four layers rather than handing each to a fresh worker.

**Completion ritual is adapted for the stack:** `/code-review-and-quality` + commit happen **per layer** (before each `gt submit`); the Linear issue moves to **Done once the whole stack is approved and merged**, not per layer. Do not push individual layers straight to `main`.

## Acceptance criteria

Observable end-to-end checks, verified after the stack merges. (`pytest` green is the per-PR-layer precondition — see Test plan — not a behavioural criterion.)

* **Headline resume.** In a throwaway repo, `worktree.add` an issue branch, commit a file, leave the worktree; run the orchestrator (with a fake/cheap model) against a stubbed cycle containing that issue → it resumes (prompt mentions prior work), completes, and the worktree is removed on Done.
* **Token budget resets.** The resumed run's log entry has a non-null, distinct `session_id` and `cumulative_tokens` reflecting only this session's spend — not the killed run's.
* **Manual escape hatch.** After `git worktree remove … && git branch -D <id>`, the next drain creates a fresh worktree (`resumed=False`) and runs normally.
* **Dirty preserved tree.** With staged + untracked edits left in the preserved worktree, drain runs and the resumed session sees them via `git status` — no `reset`/`clean` ran; the symlinked config dirs survive.
* **Leftover partial state.** With the worktree directory deleted but the branch kept, drain halts cleanly with `Halt: … — setup failed: …` (no traceback, no Linear transition).
* **Resume cap fires.** Seed 3 non-Done run-log entries for an id; the next run halts before spawn with the cap reason, `set_state` not called.
* **Cap disable.** With `max_resume_attempts: null` in `limits.yml` and ≥3 seeded non-Done entries, drain proceeds rather than halting.

## Out of scope

(Deliberate, v2 candidates.)

* **Auto-recovery of operator-induced partial states** (branch without worktree → re-checkout; orphan/prunable dir → prune). Deliberately cut for simplicity (A2): these hit the existing clean halt and the operator clears the leftover by hand. Add only if it proves needed.
* Per-resume token budget (a tighter cap for resumed sessions) — the count cap is the load-bearing guard for v1.
* Skipping a capped issue to drain the rest of the cycle (today any halt stops the run; a capped issue blocks the cycle until the operator intervenes — consistent with the existing halt contract).
* A `drain-cycle clean <id>` recovery subcommand.
* `claude --resume` session continuation (unavailable on kill; undesirable for token-breach).

## Supersedes

Replaces the "operator owns retry / detect-and-halt only" stance of <issue id="3c8d126f-d01d-4a8e-a7ac-faa69dc6027b">ABA-231</issue> (already Canceled). ### The design decision this reverses

`docs/design-decisions.md` §8 currently pins: *"Retry. A halted issue is not retried automatically. The operator inspects the worktree and decides — fix, redo manually, or descope."* This work **partially reverses** that and so must add a new **§14** that supersedes the §8 "Retry" bullet (per the "memory lives in artefacts / ADRs are superseded, not silently ignored" rule). (§13 is already taken by "Opt-in OpenTelemetry tracing to Honeycomb", which landed after this issue was shaped — the next free section number is §14; the agent should confirm the highest section at implementation time.) The reversal is narrow and must be documented as such:

* Resume reuses a **preserved, committed** work-in-progress on a **manual re-run** (or scripted re-run). It is **not** in-process auto-retry, and it is **bounded** by a resume-attempt cap (below).
* §3 (worktree preservation on halt) and §10 (`link_project_config`) are **relied upon**, not changed.
* The `docs/tasks/rerun-after-halt-detect-cleanly.md` framing ("no auto-cleanup, operator owns retry; detect-and-halt only") is superseded by this work; note it in §14. (The corresponding Linear issue, <issue id="3c8d126f-d01d-4a8e-a7ac-faa69dc6027b">ABA-231</issue>, is already Canceled/archived — this issue replaces its stance.)

### Why not `claude --resume`

A SIGKILLed worker has `session_id: null` in the run log (it dies before the terminal `result` event), so `--resume` isn't even available. And `--resume` would reload the exact context that blew the token budget, re-breaching almost immediately. The committed branch — not the session — is the correct resume point.

### Assumptions (the core bet)

* **A1.** The dominant halt is a token-cap breach, and a killed worker has usually committed incremental work to the `<id>` branch (true in the <issue id="f8927151-bb4d-4af7-9410-071b39c8577c">ABA-276</issue> case: 2 commits). If a worker dies before committing anything, resume re-runs from an empty branch — no worse than today, and bounded by the cap (A3).
* **A2.** Only the clean post-halt state (registered worktree + branch) is worth auto-handling. Operator-induced partial states (branch without worktree, orphan dir) are rare and acceptably handled by the **existing** clean halt — no new code. This is the main simplification; if partial-state auto-recovery turns out to be wanted, it's an additive follow-up.
* **A3.** A fresh session, pointed at committed work and told to continue, will build on it rather than restart. This is the core bet; the resume prompt directive (`git log`/`git status` first) is the cheap insurance, and the resume-attempt cap is the backstop if the bet fails for a given issue.

## How

### Resolved design choices

* **Resume is automatic on re-run** (the operator's explicit intent). Forcing a fresh start is the manual escape hatch: delete the worktree + branch (`git worktree remove … && git branch -D <id>`), exactly as we just did for <issue id="f8927151-bb4d-4af7-9410-071b39c8577c">ABA-276</issue>. No new flag needed.
* **Resume-attempt cap = 3** (configurable). After 3 non-Done attempts of an issue within the cycle, drain-cycle halts **before spawning** (zero token burn) and leaves the issue for manual intervention. This is the only backstop: the cycle-wide token cap does **not** fire on repeated halts — the breach path `return 1`s before `check_cycle`, and each re-run is a new process with a fresh `RunLog`, so cumulative cycle tokens reset every invocation.
* **Dirty preserved tree: preserve as-is.** The fresh session reconciles uncommitted edits via `git status`/`git log`. Never `reset --hard`/`clean` (would destroy the saved work and the gitignored config symlinks). Never switch teardown to `--force`.

### Worktree reuse logic (two branches, not five)

For picked issue `<id>` in `target_repo`, with `path = target_repo/.worktrees/<id>`:

1. **A worktree is already registered at** `path` → reuse it. Run **no git command** — just return `(path, resumed=True)`. This is the clean post-halt state (the <issue id="f8927151-bb4d-4af7-9410-071b39c8577c">ABA-276</issue> case) and the only state worth auto-handling.
2. **Otherwise** → call today's `add` (`git worktree add -b <id> <path> main`), returning `(path, resumed=False)`.

That's it. The rare leftover states — branch exists but no worktree, or an orphan directory at `path` — make `add` raise `RuntimeError` (with git's stderr), which the orchestrator's **existing** pre-spawn handler already turns into the clean `Halt: … — setup failed: …` line. We do **not** add bespoke detection, `prune`, branch re-checkout, or a new exception type for those: they're operator-induced, rare, and the operator clears the leftover by hand (`git worktree remove … && git branch -D <id>`). **The detection is one git call** (`git worktree list --porcelain`, parsed for a record at `path.resolve()`) plus the existing `add`.

Verified against real git: any `git worktree add` onto an already-registered path fails, so the reuse case must **not** call `add` — hence the registration check first. Reuse is a pure no-op on git state, so a dirty index / staged / untracked files in the preserved worktree survive untouched (this is the "preserve as-is" choice, for free).

### Component changes

#### 1\. `drain_cycle/worktree.py` — new `ensure` (keep `add` as-is)

```python
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class WorktreeHandle:
    path: Path
    resumed: bool

def ensure(repo: Path, identifier: str) -> WorktreeHandle:
    """Reuse a preserved worktree if one is registered at the expected
    path, else create a fresh one via add(). Reuse runs no git command."""
    path = repo / WORKTREE_DIR / identifier
    if _is_registered_worktree(repo, path):
        return WorktreeHandle(path, resumed=True)
    return WorktreeHandle(add(repo, identifier), resumed=False)
```

* One private helper: `_is_registered_worktree(repo, path) -> bool` — runs `git worktree list --porcelain` (via the existing `_run_git`-style call) and checks for a `worktree` record equal to `path.resolve()`. No `WorktreeStateError`, no `prune`, no branch re-checkout, no `_branch_exists`.
* **Keep** `add(repo, identifier) -> Path` **exactly as-is** — `tests/test_worktree.py::test_add_failure_raises_with_git_stderr_in_message` pins it as the fail-on-duplicate primitive that `ensure` reuses for the fresh/leftover path, and its `RuntimeError` is what the orchestrator's existing handler turns into the clean halt.
* `link_project_config` is already idempotent (`os.path.lexists` skip); call it unconditionally after `ensure` for both fresh and resumed (confirmed safe — returns `[]` on reuse).

#### 2\. `drain_cycle/prompt.py` — `resumed` kwarg

```python
def build(issue: dict[str, Any], worktree: Path, *, resumed: bool = False) -> str: ...
```

* When `resumed=True`, prepend a resume segment as the **first line of the preamble** (after the `---` separator, before "Execution instructions:") so it leads the instructions but **does not displace** `_TAIL` **from last position** (the four-segment ordering is load-bearing). Text directs the agent to run `git log --oneline main..HEAD` and `git status` first and continue rather than restart.
* `resumed=False` default keeps every current call site and all existing `tests/test_prompt.py` assertions unchanged.

#### 3\. `drain_cycle/limits.py` — `max_resume_attempts`

* Add `max_resume_attempts: int | None = 3` to the `Limits` dataclass. It is a **count**, not a resource ceiling, but lives here because `limits.yml` is the natural config home; `null` disables. The existing `_coerce`/`load` path validates it (positive number or null; rejects bool/non-positive) with no change.

#### 4\. `drain_cycle/orchestrator.py` — swap `add`→`ensure`, thread flag, add cap guard

In the pre-spawn try-block (`orchestrator.py:169-205`), replace `worktree.add(...)` with `worktree.ensure(...)` and insert the cap check **before** `linear.set_state(..., "In Progress")` (no spawn ⇒ no Linear transition, mirroring the resolution-halt contract):

```python
handle = worktree.ensure(target_repo, identifier)   # RuntimeError on leftover/conflict → existing halt
worktree_path = handle.path
worktree.link_project_config(target_repo, worktree_path, repos.worktree_config_paths)
if handle.resumed and limits.max_resume_attempts is not None \
        and _resume_attempts(log.cycle_id, identifier) >= limits.max_resume_attempts:
    # clean halt BEFORE set_state: run-log entry (cached issue state), stderr Halt line, return 1.
    # No revert (state was never moved). Distinct reason names the cap.
    ...
linear.set_state(issue["id"], _IN_PROGRESS_STATE_NAME)
...
agent_prompt = prompt.build(issue, worktree_path, resumed=handle.resumed)
```

* New `_resume_attempts(cycle_id, identifier) -> int`: glob `runlog.runs_dir()/<cycle_id>-*.json` (reusing the per-cycle filename convention) and count entries with `issue_identifier == identifier` and `final_linear_state != "Done"` (prior non-Done attempts). The current run's just-created empty file contributes nothing.
* The `resumed` flag flows **only** to `prompt.build`, never to `worker.run_issue` — the worker always spawns a fresh session (`session_id` is null on kill; `--resume` is unavailable and undesirable).
* Cap-halt reuses the `_halt_message` helper with an appended reason, so the stderr line and the run-log `halt_reason` stay single-sourced.

#### 5\. Docs

* `docs/design-decisions.md`: add **§14** (resume on re-run) documenting the reversal of the §8 Retry bullet, the state matrix, the resume cap, the dirty-tree choice, and why `--resume` is not used. Annotate the §8 Retry bullet as superseded by §14. (Confirm the next free section number at implementation time — §13 is OpenTelemetry tracing.)
* `README.md`: the halt example currently says the worktree is "left in place"; add that a re-run now **resumes** it, and document `max_resume_attempts` alongside the other `limits.yml` keys.
* `docs/tasks/rerun-after-halt-detect-cleanly.md`: note it is superseded by this feature.

### Test plan

(Mirrors existing patterns: real git in tmp repos; monkeypatched Linear + fake-claude shell script; no conftest.) `pytest` **green is the per-PR-layer precondition** (see Delivery) — the unit and integration tests below are the layer-by-layer gate, distinct from the behavioural Acceptance criteria.

`tests/test_worktree.py` (real git, existing `_init_repo`):

* fresh → `WorktreeHandle(resumed=False)`, worktree+branch created.
* reuse registered worktree → call `ensure` once, commit + stage + leave untracked files, call `ensure` again → same path, `resumed=True`, and `git status --porcelain` / `git log` unchanged (pins the no-op-on-git guarantee).
* leftover branch without worktree (operator removed the worktree) → `ensure` falls through to `add`, which raises `RuntimeError` (confirms leftover states hit the existing halt path, not a crash).
* `link_project_config` idempotent on reuse → returns `[]`, symlink intact.

`tests/test_orchestrator_resume.py` (new; mirror `test_orchestrator_iteration.py`):

* **headline**: pre-seed worktree+branch+commit for the first issue, stub it `Todo`, fake-claude marks it Done → exit 0, **no worktree-add failure**, worktree removed on Done, entry `final_linear_state == "Done"`.
* resumed prompt mentions prior work: prompt-capturing fake-claude on a pre-seeded worktree → spawned prompt contains the resume directive and `<id>`.
* resume cap: pre-seed 3 prior non-Done entries for `<id>` in `runs/<cycle-id>-*.json`, `max_resume_attempts=3` → exit 1 with the cap halt reason, `set_state` not called, no spawn.

`tests/test_prompt.py`: `resumed=True` includes the resume directive + identifier and `_TAIL` is still the last non-empty line; `resumed=False` is byte-identical to today's prompt.

`tests/test_limits.py`: `max_resume_attempts` honours absent-key default (3), explicit `null` (off), positive override, and rejects bool/non-positive via `_coerce`.

### Task breakdown

1. `worktree.py`: `WorktreeHandle`, `_is_registered_worktree`, `ensure`. `add` untouched.
2. `tests/test_worktree.py`: reuse + leftover-falls-through tests — get git mechanics green first.
3. `prompt.py` + `tests/test_prompt.py`: resume variant.
4. `limits.py` + `tests/test_limits.py`: `max_resume_attempts`.
5. `orchestrator.py`: swap `add`→`ensure`, thread `resumed`, add `_resume_attempts` + cap guard.
6. `tests/test_orchestrator_resume.py`: integration tests.
7. `docs/design-decisions.md` §14, `README.md`, `rerun-after-halt-detect-cleanly.md` note.

## Delivery — author as a 4-PR Graphite stack

**This issue is not drained via drain-cycle and is not split into Linear sub-issues.** It is authored by **one supervised agent in a single session**, working on a branch off `main` in the repo root (not a `.worktrees/` sandbox), and delivered as a **Graphite (**`gt`**) PR stack** — one PR per layer, reviewed bottom→top. This is the owner-authorised exception to the AGENTS.md "push directly to main; PRs only when the owner asks" default. <issue id="21915a3a-c443-4c9e-a032-b0964f0ed845">ABA-299</issue> stays the single tracked issue.

**Precondition:** `gt` (1.8.x) authenticated against `origin` (`github.com/ababushkin/drain-cycle`); the session is supervised, so branch/PR creation runs with approvals. The repo has **no CI**, so each layer's gate is a local `pytest` run — not a remote check.

**The stack (maps the 7 tasks above onto 4 PRs):**

| PR | Layer | Tasks | Contents | Green on its own |
| -- | -- | -- | -- | -- |
| PR1 | worktree primitive | 1, 2 | `WorktreeHandle`, `_is_registered_worktree`, `ensure`; `test_worktree.py` reuse/leftover tests. `add` untouched. | yes (its unit tests) |
| PR2 | prompt variant | 3 | `prompt.build(resumed=)` + resume segment; `test_prompt.py`. | yes |
| PR3 | limits knob | 4 | `max_resume_attempts` on `Limits`; `test_limits.py`. | yes |
| PR4 | wiring + docs | 5, 6, 7 | orchestrator `add`→`ensure`, thread `resumed`, `_resume_attempts` + cap guard; `test_orchestrator_resume.py`; **and docs (§14, README, supersession note) in this same PR**. | yes (full suite) |

PR1–3 are independent leaves (different files), each green on its own even though no production caller uses them yet. PR4 is the only behaviour-changing layer and integrates the three below it. **Docs land in PR4** so the §8-reversal ADR ships in the same PR as the behaviour that reverses it (an architectural change updates its ADR in the same PR — don't trail the docs in a later PR).

**Authoring loop — one layer at a time, gated:**

1. `gt create` a branch for the layer (conventional-commit subject, e.g. `feat: worktree.ensure reuses a preserved worktree`).
2. Run `pytest` — **the layer's own tests must be green** before submitting (no CI; local pytest is the only gate).
3. `gt submit` — opens the real GitHub PR for that layer on `origin`.
4. **STOP and report the PR; wait for the operator's go-ahead.** Do **not** start the next layer until approved.
5. On approval, build the next layer **on top** with `gt create` (never branch from `main` for layers 2–4 — the stack is one lineage; `gt` sets the parent).
6. On review feedback for a lower layer: `gt down`, amend, `gt restack`, `gt submit` to update the stack. The same agent restacks the whole tower — it has full context, which is the reason one agent authors all four layers rather than handing each to a fresh worker.

**Completion ritual is adapted for the stack:** `/code-review-and-quality` + commit happen **per layer** (before each `gt submit`); the Linear issue moves to **Done once the whole stack is approved and merged**, not per layer. Do not push individual layers straight to `main`.

## Acceptance criteria

Observable end-to-end checks, verified after the stack merges. (`pytest` green is the per-PR-layer precondition — see Test plan — not a behavioural criterion.)

* **Headline resume.** In a throwaway repo, `worktree.add` an issue branch, commit a file, leave the worktree; run the orchestrator (with a fake/cheap model) against a stubbed cycle containing that issue → it resumes (prompt mentions prior work), completes, and the worktree is removed on Done.
* **Token budget resets.** The resumed run's log entry has a non-null, distinct `session_id` and `cumulative_tokens` reflecting only this session's spend — not the killed run's.
* **Manual escape hatch.** After `git worktree remove … && git branch -D <id>`, the next drain creates a fresh worktree (`resumed=False`) and runs normally.
* **Dirty preserved tree.** With staged + untracked edits left in the preserved worktree, drain runs and the resumed session sees them via `git status` — no `reset`/`clean` ran; the symlinked config dirs survive.
* **Leftover partial state.** With the worktree directory deleted but the branch kept, drain halts cleanly with `Halt: … — setup failed: …` (no traceback, no Linear transition).
* **Resume cap fires.** Seed 3 non-Done run-log entries for an id; the next run halts before spawn with the cap reason, `set_state` not called.
* **Cap disable.** With `max_resume_attempts: null` in `limits.yml` and ≥3 seeded non-Done entries, drain proceeds rather than halting.

## Out of scope

(Deliberate, v2 candidates.)

* **Auto-recovery of operator-induced partial states** (branch without worktree → re-checkout; orphan/prunable dir → prune). Deliberately cut for simplicity (A2): these hit the existing clean halt and the operator clears the leftover by hand. Add only if it proves needed.
* Per-resume token budget (a tighter cap for resumed sessions) — the count cap is the load-bearing guard for v1.
* Skipping a capped issue to drain the rest of the cycle (today any halt stops the run; a capped issue blocks the cycle until the operator intervenes — consistent with the existing halt contract).
* A `drain-cycle clean <id>` recovery subcommand.
* `claude --resume` session continuation (unavailable on kill; undesirable for token-breach).

## Supersedes

Replaces the "operator owns retry / detect-and-halt only" stance of <issue id="3c8d126f-d01d-4a8e-a7ac-faa69dc6027b">ABA-231</issue> (already Canceled). """Thin wrapper around ``git worktree``.

Each issue gets ``.worktrees/<issue-identifier>/`` branched off ``main`` per
README §3, used once, then removed on Done.

``git worktree`` stderr is captured and surfaced in the raised
``RuntimeError`` on failure. The orchestrator's pre-spawn try/except
threads the message into the runlog's ``halt_reason`` so the operator
sees git's actual diagnostic (dirty tree, branch already exists,
missing ``main``) rather than just a non-zero exit code.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import os
import subprocess
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Iterable

from . import telemetry

BASE_BRANCH = "main"
WORKTREE_DIR = ".worktrees"


def add(repo: Path, identifier: str) -> Path:
    """Create a worktree branched off ``main`` for ``identifier``.

    Returns the absolute path to the new worktree.
    """
    worktree_path = repo / WORKTREE_DIR / identifier
    with telemetry.tracer.start_as_current_span("drain.worktree.add") as span:
        span.set_attribute("worktree.identifier", identifier)
        span.set_attribute("worktree.repo", repo.name)
        span.set_attribute("worktree.path", str(worktree_path))
        _run_git(
            ["worktree", "add", "-b", identifier, str(worktree_path), BASE_BRANCH],
            cwd=repo,
        )
    return worktree_path

"""Thin wrapper around ``git worktree``.

Each issue gets ``.worktrees/<issue-identifier>/`` branched off ``main`` per
README §3, used once, then removed on Done — or preserved on halt so a
later re-run can resume against the committed work (see
``docs/design-decisions.md`` §14).

``git worktree`` stderr is captured and surfaced in the raised
``RuntimeError`` on failure. The orchestrator's pre-spawn try/except
threads the message into the runlog's ``halt_reason`` so the operator
sees git's actual diagnostic (dirty tree, branch already exists,
missing ``main``) rather than just a non-zero exit code.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import os
import subprocess
from dataclasses import dataclass
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Iterable

from . import telemetry

BASE_BRANCH = "main"
WORKTREE_DIR = ".worktrees"


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class WorktreeHandle:
    """A prepared worktree, together with whether it was reused.

    ``resumed`` is ``True`` when ``ensure`` found a pre-existing worktree
    registered at the expected path (typically left behind by an earlier
    halted run). Callers thread the flag into the spawn-time prompt so
    the agent knows it is continuing from prior committed work rather
    than starting fresh.
    """

    path: Path
    resumed: bool


def add(repo: Path, identifier: str) -> Path:
    """Create a worktree branched off ``main`` for ``identifier``.

    Returns the absolute path to the new worktree.
    """
    worktree_path = repo / WORKTREE_DIR / identifier
    with telemetry.tracer.start_as_current_span("drain.worktree.add") as span:
        span.set_attribute("worktree.identifier", identifier)
        span.set_attribute("worktree.repo", repo.name)
        span.set_attribute("worktree.path", str(worktree_path))
        _run_git(
            ["worktree", "add", "-b", identifier, str(worktree_path), BASE_BRANCH],
            cwd=repo,
        )
    return worktree_path


def ensure(repo: Path, identifier: str) -> WorktreeHandle:
    """Reuse a preserved worktree if one is already registered, else add.

    A worktree registered at ``repo/.worktrees/<identifier>`` is reused
    as-is — no git command is run, so a dirty index, staged or untracked
    files, and the gitignored config symlinks all survive untouched. Any
    other state (no entry at that path) falls through to :func:`add`,
    whose ``RuntimeError`` on a leftover branch or orphan directory is
    what the orchestrator's existing pre-spawn handler turns into the
    clean ``Halt: … — setup failed: …`` line.
    """
    worktree_path = repo / WORKTREE_DIR / identifier
    if _is_registered_worktree(repo, worktree_path):
        return WorktreeHandle(path=worktree_path, resumed=True)
    return WorktreeHandle(path=add(repo, identifier), resumed=False)

"""Thin wrapper around ``git worktree``.

Each issue gets ``.worktrees/<issue-identifier>/`` branched off ``main`` per
README §3, used once, then removed on Done — or preserved on halt so a
later re-run can resume against the committed work (see
``docs/design-decisions.md`` §14).

``git worktree`` stderr is captured and surfaced in the raised
``RuntimeError`` on failure. The orchestrator's pre-spawn try/except
threads the message into the runlog's ``halt_reason`` so the operator
sees git's actual diagnostic (dirty tree, branch already exists,
missing ``main``) rather than just a non-zero exit code.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import os
import subprocess
from dataclasses import dataclass
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Iterable

from . import telemetry

BASE_BRANCH = "main"
WORKTREE_DIR = ".worktrees"


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class WorktreeHandle:
    """A prepared worktree, together with whether it was reused.

    ``resumed`` is ``True`` when ``ensure`` found a pre-existing worktree
    registered at the expected path (typically left behind by an earlier
    halted run). Callers thread the flag into the spawn-time prompt so
    the agent knows it is continuing from prior committed work rather
    than starting fresh.
    """

    path: Path
    resumed: bool


def add(repo: Path, identifier: str) -> Path:
    """Create a worktree branched off ``main`` for ``identifier``.

    Returns the absolute path to the new worktree.
    """
    worktree_path = repo / WORKTREE_DIR / identifier
    with telemetry.tracer.start_as_current_span("drain.worktree.add") as span:
        span.set_attribute("worktree.identifier", identifier)
        span.set_attribute("worktree.repo", repo.name)
        span.set_attribute("worktree.path", str(worktree_path))
        _run_git(
            ["worktree", "add", "-b", identifier, str(worktree_path), BASE_BRANCH],
            cwd=repo,
        )
    return worktree_path


def ensure(repo: Path, identifier: str) -> WorktreeHandle:
    """Reuse a preserved worktree if one is already registered, else add.

    A worktree registered at ``repo/.worktrees/<identifier>`` is reused
    as-is — no git command is run, so a dirty index, staged or untracked
    files, and the gitignored config symlinks all survive untouched. Any
    other state (no entry at that path) falls through to :func:`add`,
    whose ``RuntimeError`` on a leftover branch or orphan directory is
    what the orchestrator's existing pre-spawn handler turns into the
    clean ``Halt: … — setup failed: …`` line.
    """
    worktree_path = repo / WORKTREE_DIR / identifier
    if _is_registered_worktree(repo, worktree_path):
        return WorktreeHandle(path=worktree_path, resumed=True)
    return WorktreeHandle(path=add(repo, identifier), resumed=False)

"""Thin wrapper around ``git worktree``.

Each issue gets ``.worktrees/<issue-identifier>/`` branched off ``main`` per
README §3, used once, then removed on Done.

``git worktree`` stderr is captured and surfaced in the raised
``RuntimeError`` on failure. The orchestrator's pre-spawn try/except
threads the message into the runlog's ``halt_reason`` so the operator
sees git's actual diagnostic (dirty tree, branch already exists,
missing ``main``) rather than just a non-zero exit code.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import os
import subprocess
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Iterable

from . import telemetry

BASE_BRANCH = "main"
WORKTREE_DIR = ".worktrees"


def add(repo: Path, identifier: str) -> Path:
    """Create a worktree branched off ``main`` for ``identifier``.

    Returns the absolute path to the new worktree.
    """
    worktree_path = repo / WORKTREE_DIR / identifier
    with telemetry.tracer.start_as_current_span("drain.worktree.add") as span:
        span.set_attribute("worktree.identifier", identifier)
        span.set_attribute("worktree.repo", repo.name)
        span.set_attribute("worktree.path", str(worktree_path))
        _run_git(
            ["worktree", "add", "-b", identifier, str(worktree_path), BASE_BRANCH],
            cwd=repo,
        )
    return worktree_path


def link_project_config(
    repo: Path, worktree_path: Path, names: Iterable[str]
) -> list[Path]:
    """Symlink gitignored project-scoped config from ``repo`` into the worktree.

    A git worktree checks out only tracked files, so gitignored project config
    (``.claude/`` settings/hooks/agents/skills, a root ``.mcp.json``) is absent.
    Linking the repo's real entries in gives a worker the same settings, hooks,
    agents, skills, and MCP config as an interactive session at the repo root —
    and because the link points at the live dir, a stateful hook reads and
    writes the repo's actual config exactly as a non-worktree run would.

    For each name: skip it if absent in ``repo`` (a clean no-op for repos
    without that config) or if something already occupies that path in the
    worktree (a tracked entry git checked out, or a pre-existing link). The
    check uses ``os.path.lexists`` so a dangling link counts as present and is
    never clobbered. Returns the links created.
    """
    created: list[Path] = []
    repo = repo.resolve()
    for name in names:
        source = repo / name
        if not source.exists():
            continue
        link = worktree_path / name
        if os.path.lexists(link):
            continue
        os.symlink(source, link)
        created.append(link)
    return created


def remove(repo: Path, worktree_path: Path) -> None:
    """Remove a worktree previously created by :func:`add`."""
    with telemetry.tracer.start_as_current_span("drain.worktree.remove") as span:
        span.set_attribute("worktree.repo", repo.name)
        span.set_attribute("worktree.path", str(worktree_path))
        _run_git(["worktree", "remove", str(worktree_path)], cwd=repo)


def _run_git(args: list[str], *, cwd: Path) -> None:
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["git", *args],
        cwd=cwd,
        check=False,
        capture_output=True,
        text=True,
    )
    if result.returncode != 0:
        raise RuntimeError(
            f"git {' '.join(args)} failed (exit {result.returncode}): "
            f"{result.stderr.strip() or result.stdout.strip() or '<no output>'}"
        )

input.new_string:
def _is_registered_worktree(repo: Path, worktree_path: Path) -> bool:
    """Return ``True`` if git lists a worktree at ``worktree_path``.

    Parses ``git worktree list --porcelain`` for a ``worktree <path>``
    record matching ``worktree_path.resolve()``. The resolve step matches
    git's own canonicalisation (symlinks, ``..``) so a worktree under a
    symlinked repo path still matches the entry git printed.
    """
    target = worktree_path.resolve()
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["git", "worktree", "list", "--porcelain"],
        cwd=repo,
        check=False,
        capture_output=True,
        text=True,
    )
    if result.returncode != 0:
        # Treat any failure as "not registered" — the caller then falls
        # through to ``add``, whose own error surfaces git's real
        # diagnostic via the orchestrator's pre-spawn halt path.
        return False
    for line in result.stdout.splitlines():
        if not line.startswith("worktree "):
            continue
        listed = Path(line.removeprefix("worktree ").strip())
        if listed.resolve() == target:
            return True
    return False


def _run_git(args: list[str], *, cwd: Path) -> None:

            cwd=repo,
        )
    return worktree_path


def ensure(repo: Path, identifier: str) -> WorktreeHandle:
    """Reuse a preserved worktree if one is already registered, else add.

    A worktree registered at ``repo/.worktrees/<identifier>`` is reused
    as-is — no git command is run, so a dirty index, staged or untracked
    files, and the gitignored config symlinks all survive untouched. Any
    other state (no entry at that path) falls through to :func:`add`,
    whose ``RuntimeError`` on a leftover branch or orphan directory is
    what the orchestrator's existing pre-spawn handler turns into the
    clean ``Halt: … — setup failed: …`` line.
    """
    worktree_path = repo / WORKTREE_DIR / identifier
    if _is_registered_worktree(repo, worktree_path):
        return WorktreeHandle(path=worktree_path, resumed=True)
    return WorktreeHandle(path=add(repo, identifier), resumed=False)


def link_project_config(
    repo: Path, worktree_path: Path, names: Iterable[str]
) -> list[Path]:
    """Symlink gitignored project-scoped config from ``repo`` into the worktree.

    A git worktree checks out only tracked files, so gitignored project config
    (``.claude/`` settings/hooks/agents/skills, a root ``.mcp.json``) is absent.
    Linking the repo's real entries in gives a worker the same settings, hooks,
    agents, skills, and MCP config as an interactive session at the repo root —
    and because the link points at the live dir, a stateful hook reads and
    writes the repo's actual config exactly as a non-worktree run would.

    For each name: skip it if absent in ``repo`` (a clean no-op for repos
    without that config) or if something already occupies that path in the
    worktree (a tracked entry git checked out, or a pre-existing link). The
    check uses ``os.path.lexists`` so a dangling link counts as present and is
    never clobbered. Returns the links created.
    """
    created: list[Path] = []
    repo = repo.resolve()
    for name in names:
        source = repo / name
        if not source.exists():
            continue
        link = worktree_path / name
        if os.path.lexists(link):
            continue
        os.symlink(source, link)
        created.append(link)
    return created


def remove(repo: Path, worktree_path: Path) -> None:
    """Remove a worktree previously created by :func:`add`."""
    with telemetry.tracer.start_as_current_span("drain.worktree.remove") as span:
        span.set_attribute("worktree.repo", repo.name)
        span.set_attribute("worktree.path", str(worktree_path))
        _run_git(["worktree", "remove", str(worktree_path)], cwd=repo)


def _is_registered_worktree(repo: Path, worktree_path: Path) -> bool:
    """Return ``True`` if git lists a worktree at ``worktree_path``.

    Parses ``git worktree list --porcelain`` for a ``worktree <path>``
    record matching ``worktree_path.resolve()``. The resolve step matches
    git's own canonicalisation (symlinks, ``..``) so a worktree under a
    symlinked repo path still matches the entry git printed.
    """
    target = worktree_path.resolve()
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["git", "worktree", "list", "--porcelain"],
        cwd=repo,
        check=False,
        capture_output=True,
        text=True,
    )
    if result.returncode != 0:
        # Treat any failure as "not registered" — the caller then falls
        # through to ``add``, whose own error surfaces git's real
        # diagnostic via the orchestrator's pre-spawn halt path.
        return False
    for line in result.stdout.splitlines():
        if not line.startswith("worktree "):
            continue
        listed = Path(line.removeprefix("worktree ").strip())
        if listed.resolve() == target:
            return True
    return False


def _run_git(args: list[str], *, cwd: Path) -> None:
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["git", *args],
        cwd=cwd,
        check=False,
        capture_output=True,
        text=True,
    )
    if result.returncode != 0:
        raise RuntimeError(
            f"git {' '.join(args)} failed (exit {result.returncode}): "
            f"{result.stderr.strip() or result.stdout.strip() or '<no output>'}"
        )

def test_ensure_fresh_creates_worktree_and_marks_resumed_false(tmp_path: Path) -> None:
    """``ensure`` on a previously unseen identifier delegates to ``add``,
    creating the worktree+branch and returning ``resumed=False``."""
    repo = tmp_path / "repo"
    repo.mkdir()
    _init_repo(repo)

    handle = worktree.ensure(repo, "ABA-FRESH")

    assert handle.resumed is False
    assert handle.path == repo / worktree.WORKTREE_DIR / "ABA-FRESH"
    assert handle.path.is_dir()
    # The branch git just created points at HEAD on main.
    branches = subprocess.run(
        ["git", "branch", "--list", "ABA-FRESH"],
        cwd=repo,
        check=True,
        capture_output=True,
        text=True,
    ).stdout
    assert "ABA-FRESH" in branches


def test_ensure_reuse_preserves_dirty_state_and_runs_no_git_command(
    tmp_path: Path,
) -> None:
    """A second ``ensure`` for an already-registered worktree returns the
    same path with ``resumed=True``, and changes the worktree's git state
    in no observable way: a committed file, a staged file, and an
    untracked file all survive untouched. This pins the "no-op on git
    state" guarantee that the dirty-tree acceptance criterion depends on.
    """
    repo = tmp_path / "repo"
    repo.mkdir()
    _init_repo(repo)

    first = worktree.ensure(repo, "ABA-RESUME")
    assert first.resumed is False

    # Make the worktree dirty in three ways: a committed file on the
    # issue branch, a staged-but-uncommitted file, and an untracked file.
    (first.path / "committed.txt").write_text("committed\n")
    subprocess.run(
        ["git", "add", "committed.txt"],
        cwd=first.path,
        check=True,
        capture_output=True,
    )
    subprocess.run(
        ["git", "commit", "-m", "prior commit"],
        cwd=first.path,
        check=True,
        capture_output=True,
    )
    (first.path / "staged.txt").write_text("staged\n")
    subprocess.run(
        ["git", "add", "staged.txt"],
        cwd=first.path,
        check=True,
        capture_output=True,
    )
    (first.path / "untracked.txt").write_text("untracked\n")

    status_before = subprocess.run(
        ["git", "status", "--porcelain"],
        cwd=first.path,
        check=True,
        capture_output=True,
        text=True,
    ).stdout
    log_before = subprocess.run(
        ["git", "log", "--oneline"],
        cwd=first.path,
        check=True,
        capture_output=True,
        text=True,
    ).stdout

    second = worktree.ensure(repo, "ABA-RESUME")

    assert second.resumed is True
    assert second.path == first.path
    # Status + log are byte-identical: ``ensure`` ran no mutating git command.
    status_after = subprocess.run(
        ["git", "status", "--porcelain"],
        cwd=first.path,
        check=True,
        capture_output=True,
        text=True,
    ).stdout
    log_after = subprocess.run(
        ["git", "log", "--oneline"],
        cwd=first.path,
        check=True,
        capture_output=True,
        text=True,
    ).stdout
    assert status_after == status_before
    assert log_after == log_before
    # All three dirty-state artefacts still exist.
    assert (first.path / "committed.txt").read_text() == "committed\n"
    assert (first.path / "staged.txt").read_text() == "staged\n"
    assert (first.path / "untracked.txt").read_text() == "untracked\n"


def test_ensure_leftover_branch_without_worktree_falls_through_to_add(
    tmp_path: Path,
) -> None:
    """If the operator deletes the worktree directory but leaves the
    branch behind, ``ensure`` does not auto-recover — it falls through
    to ``add``, which raises ``RuntimeError`` (the orchestrator's halt
    path)."""
    repo = tmp_path / "repo"
    repo.mkdir()
    _init_repo(repo)

    handle = worktree.ensure(repo, "ABA-LEFT")
    # Simulate the partial-cleanup state: worktree gone, branch kept.
    subprocess.run(
        ["git", "worktree", "remove", str(handle.path)],
        cwd=repo,
        check=True,
        capture_output=True,
    )
    branches = subprocess.run(
        ["git", "branch", "--list", "ABA-LEFT"],
        cwd=repo,
        check=True,
        capture_output=True,
        text=True,
    ).stdout

    assert "worktree add" in msg
    assert "ABA-LEFT" in msg


def test_ensure_link_project_config_idempotent_on_reuse(tmp_path: Path) -> None:

    assert first_links == [handle.path / ".claude"]

    assert reused.resumed is True

    assert second_links == []
    assert (handle.path / ".claude").is_symlink()
    assert Path(os.readlink(handle.path / ".claude")) == repo.resolve() / ".claude"

"""Tests for the ``worktree`` git wrapper.

The happy-path is exercised end-to-end by ``test_orchestrator_iteration``.
What these tests pin is the failure mode: git's stderr must be captured
and surfaced in the raised ``RuntimeError`` so the operator (and the
runlog ``halt_reason`` written by the orchestrator) sees what actually
went wrong, not just a non-zero exit code.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import os
import subprocess
from pathlib import Path

import pytest

from drain_cycle import worktree


def _init_repo(repo: Path) -> None:
    subprocess.run(["git", "init", "-b", "main"], cwd=repo, check=True, capture_output=True)
    subprocess.run(["git", "config", "user.email", "t@x"], cwd=repo, check=True)
    subprocess.run(["git", "config", "user.name", "T"], cwd=repo, check=True)
    (repo / "README.md").write_text("seed\n")
    subprocess.run(["git", "add", "."], cwd=repo, check=True, capture_output=True)
    subprocess.run(["git", "commit", "-m", "seed"], cwd=repo, check=True, capture_output=True)


def _commit_gitignore(repo: Path, *patterns: str) -> None:
    (repo / ".gitignore").write_text("\n".join(patterns) + "\n")
    subprocess.run(["git", "add", ".gitignore"], cwd=repo, check=True, capture_output=True)
    subprocess.run(
        ["git", "commit", "-m", "gitignore"], cwd=repo, check=True, capture_output=True
    )


def test_add_failure_raises_with_git_stderr_in_message(tmp_path: Path) -> None:
    """``git worktree add`` fails when the branch name is already taken.
    The raised RuntimeError must carry git's stderr so the operator can
    diagnose without re-running by hand."""
    repo = tmp_path / "repo"
    repo.mkdir()
    _init_repo(repo)

    # First add succeeds, taking the branch "ABA-X".
    worktree.add(repo, "ABA-X")
    # Second add fails — same branch.
    with pytest.raises(RuntimeError) as excinfo:
        worktree.add(repo, "ABA-X")

    msg = str(excinfo.value)
    # The message names the operation and includes git's actual complaint
    # (typically "already exists" or "already checked out").
    assert "worktree add" in msg
    assert "ABA-X" in msg


def test_remove_failure_raises_with_git_stderr_in_message(tmp_path: Path) -> None:
    """``git worktree remove`` on a non-existent path fails. The raised
    RuntimeError must carry git's stderr."""
    repo = tmp_path / "repo"
    repo.mkdir()
    _init_repo(repo)

    bogus = repo / ".worktrees" / "never-existed"
    with pytest.raises(RuntimeError) as excinfo:
        worktree.remove(repo, bogus)

    msg = str(excinfo.value)
    assert "worktree remove" in msg
    # Either git's own "is not a working tree" message or the path itself.
    assert str(bogus) in msg or "working tree" in msg.lower()


def test_link_project_config_symlinks_gitignored_claude(tmp_path: Path) -> None:
    """A gitignored ``.claude/`` (absent from the worktree checkout) is
    symlinked back to the repo's real dir, so the worker reads through it."""
    repo = tmp_path / "repo"
    repo.mkdir()
    _init_repo(repo)
    _commit_gitignore(repo, ".claude", ".worktrees")
    (repo / ".claude").mkdir()
    (repo / ".claude" / "settings.json").write_text('{"hooks": {}}\n')

    wt = worktree.add(repo, "ABA-A")
    created = worktree.link_project_config(repo, wt, [".claude"])

    assert created == [wt / ".claude"]
    assert (wt / ".claude").is_symlink()
    assert Path(os.readlink(wt / ".claude")) == repo.resolve() / ".claude"
    assert (wt / ".claude" / "settings.json").read_text() == '{"hooks": {}}\n'


def test_link_project_config_noop_when_no_claude(tmp_path: Path) -> None:
    """A repo without the named config produces no link and no error."""
    repo = tmp_path / "repo"
    repo.mkdir()
    _init_repo(repo)
    _commit_gitignore(repo, ".worktrees")

    wt = worktree.add(repo, "ABA-A")
    created = worktree.link_project_config(repo, wt, [".claude", ".mcp.json"])

    assert created == []
    assert not os.path.lexists(wt / ".claude")
    assert not os.path.lexists(wt / ".mcp.json")


def test_link_project_config_skips_existing_tracked_entry(tmp_path: Path) -> None:
    """A tracked ``.claude/`` git already checked out is left as a real dir,
    never clobbered by a symlink."""
    repo = tmp_path / "repo"
    repo.mkdir()
    _init_repo(repo)
    (repo / ".claude").mkdir()
    (repo / ".claude" / "settings.json").write_text("tracked\n")
    subprocess.run(["git", "add", ".claude"], cwd=repo, check=True, capture_output=True)
    subprocess.run(
        ["git", "commit", "-m", "track claude"], cwd=repo, check=True, capture_output=True
    )

    wt = worktree.add(repo, "ABA-A")
    created = worktree.link_project_config(repo, wt, [".claude"])

    assert created == []
    assert not (wt / ".claude").is_symlink()
    assert (wt / ".claude" / "settings.json").read_text() == "tracked\n"


def test_link_project_config_includes_mcp_and_entire_when_present(
    tmp_path: Path,
) -> None:
    """Every present, gitignored name is linked; absent ones are skipped."""
    repo = tmp_path / "repo"
    repo.mkdir()
    _init_repo(repo)
    _commit_gitignore(repo, ".claude", ".mcp.json", ".entire", ".worktrees")
    (repo / ".mcp.json").write_text("{}\n")
    (repo / ".entire").mkdir()
    (repo / ".entire" / "state").write_text("x\n")

    wt = worktree.add(repo, "ABA-A")
    created = worktree.link_project_config(
        repo, wt, [".claude", ".mcp.json", ".entire"]
    )

    assert set(created) == {wt / ".mcp.json", wt / ".entire"}
    assert (wt / ".mcp.json").is_symlink()
    assert (wt / ".entire").is_symlink()
    assert not os.path.lexists(wt / ".claude")


def test_linked_config_is_gitignored_in_worktree(tmp_path: Path) -> None:
    """The linked entry is gitignored in the worktree (it shares the repo's
    tracked .gitignore), so a worker's ``git add`` never stages it."""
    repo = tmp_path / "repo"
    repo.mkdir()
    _init_repo(repo)
    _commit_gitignore(repo, ".claude", ".worktrees")
    (repo / ".claude").mkdir()
    (repo / ".claude" / "settings.json").write_text("{}\n")

    wt = worktree.add(repo, "ABA-A")
    worktree.link_project_config(repo, wt, [".claude"])

    status = subprocess.run(
        ["git", "status", "--porcelain"],
        cwd=wt,
        check=True,
        capture_output=True,
        text=True,
    )
    assert ".claude" not in status.stdout


def test_remove_preserves_symlink_target(tmp_path: Path) -> None:
    """Removing the worktree deletes the symlink, not the repo's real dir."""
    repo = tmp_path / "repo"
    repo.mkdir()
    _init_repo(repo)
    _commit_gitignore(repo, ".claude", ".worktrees")
    (repo / ".claude").mkdir()
    (repo / ".claude" / "settings.json").write_text("keep me\n")

    wt = worktree.add(repo, "ABA-A")
    worktree.link_project_config(repo, wt, [".claude"])
    worktree.remove(repo, wt)

    assert (repo / ".claude").is_dir()
    assert (repo / ".claude" / "settings.json").read_text() == "keep me\n"

+    # Simulate the partial-cleanup state: worktree gone, branch kept.
+    assert "ABA-LEFT" in branches  # branch survived the partial cleanup
+    """A second ``link_project_config`` on a resumed worktree returns
+    an empty list (every name already lexists) and leaves the existing
+    symlink intact — confirming the orchestrator can call it
+    unconditionally after both fresh and reused ensures."""
+    # Second call on the reused worktree returns nothing and the link
+    # is still pointing at the same target.
"""Removing the worktree deletes the symlink, not the repo's real dir."""

Five-axis code review of the working-tree diff in /Users/anton/src/drain-cycle (branch feat-worktree-ensure off main). This is PR1 of a 4-PR Graphite stack for Linear issue ABA-299 ("Resume a halted issue on re-run").

Context:
- The repo is `drain-cycle`, a single-user CLI that drains a Linear cycle by spawning `claude -p` workers in per-issue git worktrees. The orchestrator preserves worktrees on halt; today a re-run fails opaquely because `git worktree add -b <id>` clashes with the leftover branch. PR1 adds the primitive that lets a later PR (PR4) reuse the preserved worktree instead.

Scope of PR1 (changes in this diff only):
- `drain_cycle/worktree.py`: new `WorktreeHandle(path, resumed)` frozen dataclass; new public `ensure(repo, identifier) -> WorktreeHandle` (reuse if registered, else fall through to existing `add`); new private `_is_registered_worktree(repo, worktree_path) -> bool` (parses `git worktree list --porcelain`). `add(...)` is intentionally unchanged.
- `tests/test_worktree.py`: 4 new tests against real git in tmp repos — fresh ensure, dirty-reuse no-op-on-git-state, leftover-branch-without-worktree falls through to add, link_project_config idempotent on reuse.

What I want you to evaluate:
1. Correctness — does `_is_registered_worktree` parse `git worktree list --porcelain` safely? Edge cases: paths with spaces, repo at a symlinked path, non-zero exit (e.g. corrupt worktrees), Windows-style separators (we only care about macOS/Linux). Are there scenarios where the reuse path would silently destroy committed work?
2. Readability — module docstring/comments, public-API surface, dataclass shape.
3. Architecture — is `ensure` the right seam, or should the orchestrator inspect+branch? Is keeping `add` untouched the right call?
4. Security — minor here; the function shells out to git in repo paths the operator owns. Anything obvious?
5. Performance — `git worktree list --porcelain` runs on every ensure; acceptable in the cycle loop?

Also call out anything that conflicts with the issue's design ("two branches, not five"; "no auto-recovery of partial states"; "reuse runs no git command").

Report Critical / Required / Suggested findings with line numbers. Under 600 words. The full issue body is at https://linear.app/ababushkin/issue/ABA-299 — work from the diff if you don't have it.

content.text:
Good — `resolve()` is non-strict by default, so missing paths still canonicalize. And note the `/tmp` → `/private/tmp` symlink resolution on macOS, which is exactly the symlinked-repo-path case the code handles.

## Review Summary

**Verdict:** APPROVE (with one Important nit worth fixing before merge)

**Overview:** Small, well-scoped PR1 that introduces `ensure()` + `WorktreeHandle` as the reuse seam, leaves `add()` untouched, and pins the no-op-on-reuse contract with tests against real git. The implementation respects the issue's two-branch design ("registered → reuse; everything else → fall through to add"), runs no recovery commands, and surfaces git's real diagnostic via the existing halt path. Tests are tight and assert byte-identical `status`/`log` before and after — strong evidence for the no-op claim.

### Critical Issues
- None.

### Important Issues
- `drain_cycle/worktree.py:138-143` — Porcelain parsing strips the `worktree ` prefix and then calls `.strip()` on the remainder. If a path ever contains a trailing space (legal in POSIX) or, worse, embedded `\n`, this loses information. More importantly, `git worktree list --porcelain` since git 2.48 emits a `worktree-format-version 1` directive that uses the **`-z`** form when paths contain unsafe characters — without `-z`, paths with newlines are not representable and git silently truncates. For the cycle loop today the operator owns the paths (`.worktrees/<identifier>`, identifiers are Linear IDs like `ABA-299`) so this is theoretical, but the fix is one flag: pass `-z` and split on `\0`, or — simpler — sidestep parsing entirely by checking `(worktree_path / ".git").exists()` and confirming with `git -C <wt> rev-parse --is-inside-work-tree`. The current parser is correct for every identifier this repo will ever generate; flag is for the next reader who copies this code to a context with looser identifiers.

### Suggestions
- `drain_cycle/worktree.py:60-74` — `ensure()` has no telemetry span while `add()` and `remove()` do. A `drain.worktree.ensure` span with a `worktree.resumed` attribute would let Honeycomb show resume rate over time — directly answers "is the resume primitive being exercised in real drains?" Cheap to add and matches the recent OTel commit's intent.
- `drain_cycle/worktree.py:117` — Docstring says "Parses `git worktree list --porcelain` for a `worktree <path>` record" — accurate, but doesn't mention the swallow-nonzero-exit branch. One extra sentence ("A non-zero exit is treated as not-registered so `add()` runs and surfaces git's real error") would put the load-bearing behaviour in the docstring next to the code.
- `tests/test_worktree.py:316-340` — Test name says "link_project_config idempotent on reuse" but the test also asserts `reused.resumed is True`. Two assertions in one test is fine; consider renaming to `test_ensure_reuse_then_link_project_config_is_idempotent` so the intent reads off the name.
- Consider exporting `WorktreeHandle` from `drain_cycle/__init__.py` if PR2/3 will import it; if not, ignore.

### What's Done Well
- The dirty-state test (`test_ensure_reuse_preserves_dirty_state_and_runs_no_git_command`) is the right test: it captures `git status --porcelain` and `git log --oneline` byte-strings before and after and asserts equality. That's exactly the "no git command run" contract from the design doc, expressed as observable evidence rather than as a mock assertion.
- The leftover-branch-without-worktree case is explicitly tested and asserts `RuntimeError` propagation with both `"worktree add"` and the identifier in the message — pins the "no auto-recovery" rule and the orchestrator's halt-line contract in one assertion.
- `add()` is genuinely untouched. The new seam is `ensure()`; existing callers can migrate in PR2/3 without churn.
- `Path.resolve()` on both sides of the comparison correctly handles the symlinked-repo-path case (verified: macOS `/tmp` → `/private/tmp`).

### Design-doc conformance
- "Two branches, not five": yes — `registered → reuse` / `not registered → add`.
- "No auto-recovery of partial states": yes — leftover branch without worktree falls through to `add()` and raises (tested).
- "Reuse runs no git command": yes — the only git call on the reuse path is `git worktree list --porcelain`, which is read-only and observably non-mutating (tested).

### Verification Story
- Tests reviewed: yes — 4 new tests, all asserting observable git state rather than mocks. 12/12 pass locally (`uv run pytest tests/test_worktree.py`).
- Build verified: yes — full worktree test module green.
- Security checked: yes — `subprocess.run` is called with a list (no shell), `cwd=repo` is operator-owned, no untrusted input flows to git. Nothing actionable.

### Relevant files
- /Users/anton/src/drain-cycle/drain_cycle/worktree.py
- /Users/anton/src/drain-cycle/tests/test_worktree.py

content.text: The review was APPROVE with one Important nit (porcelain parsing) and helpful suggestions. Applying the porcelain `-z` fix, adding a telemetry span for `ensure`, and improving the helper docstring.

input.old_string:
def ensure(repo: Path, identifier: str) -> WorktreeHandle:
    """Reuse a preserved worktree if one is already registered, else add.

    A worktree registered at ``repo/.worktrees/<identifier>`` is reused
    as-is — no git command is run, so a dirty index, staged or untracked
    files, and the gitignored config symlinks all survive untouched. Any
    other state (no entry at that path) falls through to :func:`add`,
    whose ``RuntimeError`` on a leftover branch or orphan directory is
    what the orchestrator's existing pre-spawn handler turns into the
    clean ``Halt: … — setup failed: …`` line.
    """
    worktree_path = repo / WORKTREE_DIR / identifier
    if _is_registered_worktree(repo, worktree_path):
        return WorktreeHandle(path=worktree_path, resumed=True)
    return WorktreeHandle(path=add(repo, identifier), resumed=False)
input.new_string:
def ensure(repo: Path, identifier: str) -> WorktreeHandle:
    """Reuse a preserved worktree if one is already registered, else add.

    A worktree registered at ``repo/.worktrees/<identifier>`` is reused
    as-is — no mutating git command is run, so a dirty index, staged or
    untracked files, and the gitignored config symlinks all survive
    untouched. Any other state (no entry at that path) falls through to
    :func:`add`, whose ``RuntimeError`` on a leftover branch or orphan
    directory is what the orchestrator's existing pre-spawn handler
    turns into the clean ``Halt: … — setup failed: …`` line.
    """
    worktree_path = repo / WORKTREE_DIR / identifier
    with telemetry.tracer.start_as_current_span("drain.worktree.ensure") as span:
        span.set_attribute("worktree.identifier", identifier)
        span.set_attribute("worktree.repo", repo.name)
        span.set_attribute("worktree.path", str(worktree_path))
        if _is_registered_worktree(repo, worktree_path):
            span.set_attribute("worktree.resumed", True)
            return WorktreeHandle(path=worktree_path, resumed=True)
        span.set_attribute("worktree.resumed", False)
    return WorktreeHandle(path=add(repo, identifier), resumed=False)
